mnemonics.title: Password Strength Rules
mnemonics.content: LONG + COMPLEX = SECURE - Longer passwords with uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols are more secure

acronyms.usage: The three authentication factors: password/token (knowledge/possession), fingerprint (inherence)
One authentication grants access to multiple applications
A password valid for a single login session

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a one-way grinder: you put a password in, and it gets completely destroyed into unique fingerprints (hash) that can never be reassembled back into the original password
Think of salt as unique seasoning added to each password before 'grinding' - even identical passwords (plain steaks) become completely different dishes (hashes)
A giant cookbook of pre-made hash-to-password pairs that attackers flip through to find matches - salting spoils this by creating unique recipes

analogies.analogy: Authentication is like showing your ID at the door (proving who you are), while authorization is like having a VIP wristband that determines which rooms you can enter
Hashing is like a fingerprint - unique to each person (password), impossible to reverse engineer a person from a fingerprint, but you can verify identity by comparing fingerprints
Like trying every possible combination on a combination lock - guaranteed to work eventually but could take years for complex locks
